author: Jonathan Gray, Liliana Bounegru, Lucy Chambers (eds.)

extracts.extract: Data can be the source of data journalism, or it can be the tool with which the story is told—or it can be both. Like any source, it should be treated with skepticism; and like any tool, we should be conscious of how it can shape and restrict the stories that are created with it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Data analysis can reveal 'a story's shape' (Sarah Cohen), or provides us with a 'new camera' (David McCandless). By using data, the job of journalists shifts its main focus from being the first ones to report to being the ones telling us what a certain development might actually mean.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Becoming knowledgeable in searching, cleaning, and visualizing data is transformative for the profession of information gathering. Journalists who master this will experience that building articles on facts and insights is a relief. Less guessing, less looking for quotes; instead, a journalist can build a strong position supported by data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: When information was scarce, most of our efforts were devoted to hunting and gathering. Now that information is abundant, processing is more important. We process at two levels: 1) analysis to bring sense and structure out of the never-ending flow of data and 2) presentation to get what's important and relevant into the consumer's head. Like science, data journalism discloses its methods and presents its findings in a way that can be verified by replication.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Figures are more likely to be taken at face value than other facts, as they carry an aura of seriousness even when they are entirely fabricated. Fluency with data will help journalists sharpen their critical sense when faced with numbers and will hopefully help them gain back some terrain in their exchanges with PR departments.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Journalists need to have access to raw data, and to learn not to rely on official interpretations of it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: The exercise should not be about just analyzing or visualizing data for the sake of it, but to use it as a tool to get closer to the truth of what is going on in the world. I see the ability to be able to analyze and interpret data as an essential part of today's journalists' toolkit, rather than a separate discipline.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Ideally, you use the data to pinpoint outliers, areas of interest, or things that are surprising. In this sense, data can act as a lead or a tip off. While numbers can be interesting, just writing about the data is not enough. You still need to do the reporting to explain what it means.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Precision journalism was envisioned to be practiced in mainstream media institutions by professionals trained in journalism and social sciences. It was born in response to 'new journalism,' a form of journalism in which fiction techniques were applied to reporting. Meyer suggests that scientific techniques of data collection and analysis, rather than literary techniques, are what is needed for journalism to accomplish its search for objectivity and truth.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: CAR is a technique for gathering and analyzing data as a way of enhancing (usually investigative) reportage, whereas data journalism pays attention to the way that data sits within the whole journalistic workflow. In this sense data journalism pays as much—and sometimes more—attention to the data itself, rather than using data simply as a means to find or enhance stories.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: By enabling anyone to drill down into data sources and find information that is relevant to them, as well as to verify assertions and challenge commonly received assumptions, data journalism effectively represents the mass democratization of resources, tools, techniques, and methodologies that were previously used by specialists.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Co-location of the team is vital. Our developer and designer were offsite and came in for meetings. This is definitely not optimal\! Place everyone in the same room as the journalists.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The ABC data journalism team comprised: a web developer and designer, a lead journalist, a part-time researcher with expertise in data extraction, Excel spreadsheets, and data cleaning, a part-time junior journalist, a consultant executive producer, an academic consultant with expertise in data mining, graphic visualization, and advanced research skills, and a reference group of journalists consulted on an as-needed basis.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Data journalism at the BBC broadly covers projects that use data to do one or more of the following: enable a reader to discover information that is personally relevant; reveal a story that is remarkable and previously unknown; help the reader to better understand a complex issue.
extracts.notes: Three purposes of data journalism at BBC
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: In this area, we have found it most productive to partner with investigative teams or programs that have the expertise and time to investigate a story. As well as partnering with investigative journalists, having access to numerous journalists with specialist knowledge is essential.
extracts.notes: BBC approach to mining data for stories
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The team that produces data journalism for the BBC News website is comprised of about 20 journalists, designers, and developers. We don't have people who are specifically identified as data journalists, but all editorial staff on the team have to be proficient at using basic spreadsheet applications such as Excel and Google Docs to analyze data. While we are all either a journalist, designer, or developer 'first,' we continue to work hard to increase our understanding and proficiency in each other's areas of expertise.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The core products for exploring data are Excel, Google Docs, and Fusion Tables. The team has also used MySQL, Access databases, and Solr to explore larger datasets; and used RDF and SPARQL to begin looking at ways in which we can model events using Linked Data technologies. Developers also use their programming language of choice, whether that's ActionScript, Python, or Perl, to match, parse, or generally pick apart a dataset.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: It's important that we sit in the newsroom. We usually find work via face-to-face conversations with reporters. They know that we're happy to help write a screen scraper for a crummy government website, tear up a stack of PDFs, or otherwise turn non-data into something you can analyze. It's sort of our team's loss leader; this way we find out about potential data projects at the outset.
extracts.chapter: How the News Apps Team at the Chicago Tribune Works
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: We work in an agile fashion. Every morning begins with a 5-minute stand-up meeting. We frequently program in pairs; two developers at one keyboard are often more productive than two developers at two keyboards. Most projects don't take more than a week to produce, but on longer projects we work in week-long iterations, and show our work to stakeholders every week. 'Fail fast' is the mantra.
extracts.chapter: How the News Apps Team at the Chicago Tribune Works
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Our work sings when it helps a reader find his or her own story in the data. The written piece will speak to the trend and humanize it with a few anecdotes. But what's the reader to do when they've finished the story? Is their family safe? Are their children being educated properly?
extracts.chapter: How the News Apps Team at the Chicago Tribune Works
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Firstly, we locate the data or receive it from a variety of sources. We then start looking at what we can do with the data; do we need to mash it up with another dataset? How can we show changes over time? Those spreadsheets often have to be seriously tidied up—all those extraneous columns and weirdly merged cells really don't help. And that's assuming it's not a PDF, the worst format for data known to humankind.
extracts.chapter: Behind the Scenes at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.notes: Simon Rogers on Guardian Datablog process
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Often official data comes with the official codes added in; each school, hospital, constituency, and local authority has a unique identifier code. Countries have them too. They're useful because you may want to start mashing datasets together, and it's amazing how many different spellings and word arrangements can get in the way of that. Codes allow us to compare like with like.
extracts.chapter: Behind the Scenes at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Numbers without analysis are just numbers, which is where we fit in. When Britain's prime minister claims the riots in August 2011 were not about poverty, we were able to map the addresses of the rioters with poverty indicators to show the truth behind the claim.
extracts.chapter: Behind the Scenes at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: When dealing with deadlines, it's better to be a jack of all trades than a master of one. News apps require data wrangling, dynamic graphics, and derring-do. Holistic thinking favors narrative value over technical detail. Building things fast requires mixed teams working towards common goals.
extracts.notes: Alastair Dant, Guardian, on what makes a good news hacker
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: It's not enough to just hire developers. You need technical management. A lone-gun developer (especially fresh from journalism school, with no industry experience) is going to make many bad decisions. Even the best programmer, when left to her own devices, will choose technically interesting work over doing what's most important to your audience.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Think outside your country. In many instances, it is much easier to get information from abroad than from within the country where the investigative journalist operates. Information gathered from abroad via foreign information databases or by using other countries' access to information laws might be just what you need to put the investigative puzzle together.
extracts.chapter: Following the Money: Data Journalism and Cross-Border Collaboration
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Make use of existing investigative journalism networks. Journalists can make use of professional journalism platforms where global journalism related information is exchanged on a daily basis. Many of the reporters grouped in networks work on similar issues and confront similar situations, so it makes a lot of sense to exchange information and methods.
extracts.chapter: Following the Money: Data Journalism and Cross-Border Collaboration
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Our job as data journalists is to provide the framework or environment for this. As well as the coding and data management bits, we have to think of clever ways to design experiences. The User Experience (UX) derives mostly from the (Graphical) User Interface (GUI). In the end, this is the part which will make or break a project.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Data projects don't date. Depending on their design, new material can be added to data journalism apps. You can build on your past work: when undertaking a data project, you will often create bits of code that can be reused or updated. The next project might take half the time.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The approach requires not only programming skills, but also an understanding of what kind of information can make a story with impact. It can safely be said that anyone who wishes to imitate their service would probably have to acquire those two skill sets through partnership, because individuals rarely possess both.
extracts.chapter: Kaas & Mulvad: Semi-Finished Content for Stakeholder Groups
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The first step should be to look for immediate opportunities close to home: low-hanging fruit. For example, you might already have collections of structured texts and data that you could use. A prime example is the Homicide database of the Los Angeles Times. Here, data and visualizations are the core, not an afterthought. The editors collect all the crimes they find and only then write articles based on this.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: The project really required our combined skills: deep domain knowledge, an understanding of data best practices, design and coding skills. More importantly it required an ability to find the story in the data. It also took editing, not only for the story that went with it, but for the news app itself.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: We worked really hard at making sure the app told a 'far' story and a 'near' story. That is, the app needed to present the reader with a broad, abstract national picture; a way to compare how states did relative to each other. But given that abstraction sometimes leaves readers confused as to what the data means to them, we also wanted readers to be able to find their own local school and compare it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: You have to know the material and be inquisitive\! All of the rules that apply to other kinds of journalism apply here. You have to get the facts right, make sure you tell the story well, and—crucially—make sure your news app doesn't disagree with a story you're writing. If it does, one of the two might be wrong.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Before you tackle a project of this level of effort, you have to be certain that the findings are original, and that you will end up with good stories nobody else has. The process was broken up into a number of distinct steps: 1. Identify who keeps the data and how it is kept. 2. Download and prepare the data. 3. Create a database. 4. Double-checking and analysis.
extracts.chapter: A Nine Month Investigation into European Structural Funds
extracts.notes: Cynthia O'Murchu, FT/BIJ EU Structural Funds
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Our biggest task was extracting data out of PDFs, some hundreds of pages long. Much of this was done using UnPDF and ABBYY FineReader, which allow data to be extracted to formats such as CSV or Excel. It also involved checking and double-checking that the PDF extraction tools had captured the data correctly. This was done using filtering, sorting, and summing up totals to ensure it corresponded with what was printed on the PDFs.
extracts.chapter: A Nine Month Investigation into European Structural Funds
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: With help of Google Translate, which was plugged into the search functionality of our database, keywords would be translated into 21 languages and would return appropriate results. These could be downloaded and reporters could do further research on the individual projects of interest.
extracts.chapter: A Nine Month Investigation into European Structural Funds
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: Sometimes I spent hours gathering information only to find out that it told me, well, nothing. That I had dug up the entirely wrong set of numbers. Sometimes the data were just too old. Mornings I'd export data into spreadsheets and make charts to see trends. Afternoons I'd look at those data in clusters, putting the countries against each other to find stories.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Journalists are often not used to working with raw data, and many don't consider it a necessary foundation for their reporting. Analyzing and understanding data is a time-intensive process, even with the necessary skills. Fitting this into a short-lived news cycle is hard, so data journalism is often used in longer-term, investigative projects.
extracts.chapter: Covering the Public Purse with OpenSpending.org
extracts.notes: Lucy Chambers and Jonathan Gray, OKF
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Data released by governments is often incomplete or outdated. Very often, public databases cannot be used for investigative purposes without the addition of more specific pieces of information requested through FOI. Advocacy groups, scholars, and researchers often have more time and resources to conduct more extensive data-driven research than journalists. It can be very fruitful to team up with them.
extracts.chapter: Covering the Public Purse with OpenSpending.org
extracts.notes: Lucy Chambers and Jonathan Gray, OKF
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: The tools we used for the project were Excel and Google Refine for data cleaning and analysis; Cytoscape for network analysis; and Illustrator and Flash for the visualizations. The most important lesson was that data structures can be very hard. If the original data is not in suitable format, recalculating and converting it will take a lot of time.
extracts.chapter: Finnish Parliamentary Elections and Campaign Funding
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: Almost all projects stalled when searching for information. Most of the time, they began with a journalistic question. But then? What data is available? Where can you find it? And when you find this data, can you answer your question with it? Journalists usually know where they can find information when doing research for an article. With data journalism, most journalists don't know what information is available.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: For data journalism to work, we have to integrate it in the newsroom. Journalists have to think in data, in addition to quotes, press releases, council meetings, and so on.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Our team built a simple internal database using SQL. Reporters could now search stories for key words or events. Suddenly the dataset became accessible and generating stories became easier. We also started filtering the data to help us tell one of the key stories of the war.
extracts.notes: Simon Rogers on processing Afghan war logs
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: We worked with the reporters and editor from the education team to choose the interesting data. There's a lot of data out there that seems interesting but which a reporter will tell you is actually flawed or misleading. We also surveyed and interviewed folks with school-age kids in our newsroom. We did this because of an empathy gap—nobody on the news apps team has school-age kids.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: The graphics desk is your friend. They're good at making complex information digestible. Ask the newsroom for help—a newsroom-wide survey and interviews is a great way to get the opinion of thoughtful people who, like our audience, are diverse in background. Show your work\! Much of our feedback has been requests for the data that the application used.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Stories like these become possible when you use data to produce evidence to test independently allegations being made by sources who may have their own agendas. These stories also are a good example of the necessity for strong public records laws.
extracts.notes: Steve Doig on California hospital billing investigation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Make sure you keep notes on how you manipulate the original data. Keep a copy of the original data and never change the original. Check and double-check the data. Do the analysis several times (if need be, from scratch).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: The data was given to us as a 1,200 page PDF document, which we then had to convert into a spreadsheet to do the analysis. As well as reporting our conclusions, we published this Excel spreadsheet on the BBC News website along with our story. This gave everyone else access to the data in a usable form. Others then used this data for their own analyses. We added links to these sites to our online news story.
extracts.chapter: Which Car Model? MOT Failure Rates
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: It's part of incorporating accountability and transparency into the journalistic process. Others can understand your methods and check your work if they want to. Other people may spot points of significant interest which you've missed, or simply details that matter to them even if they weren't important enough to feature in your story.
extracts.chapter: Which Car Model? MOT Failure Rates
extracts.notes: Martin Rosenbaum, BBC, on why to release data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: We teamed up with a senior programmer to develop a scraper in order to automate the regular download and conversion of these PDFs into Excel and Database files. We developed a normalizer to allow us to reconcile bus company names and cross-reference the two datasets. We reverse engineered the license plates for buses belonging to all listed companies to find the average age of buses per company.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Crowdsourcing works best when users do a data collection or data-refining task. We pondered whether to ask users for a scan of the original bill, thinking it would deter some of them. While it might have put off some, it increased the credibility of the data. We designed a point system and a peer-review mechanism to vet user contributions.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: When searching for data, make sure that you include both search terms relating to the content of the data you're trying to find, as well as some information on the format or source. Google and other search engines allow you to search by file type. For example, you can look only for spreadsheets (by appending your search with 'filetype:XLS filetype:CSV'), geodata ('filetype:shp'), or database extracts ('filetype:MDB, filetype:SQL, filetype:DB').
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: The first trick I use in getting hold of data held by a public entity is to try to go directly to the data holder, not the public affairs person, not through an FOIA. If I first successfully reach the person who handles data for that organization, I can ask questions about what data they keep on the subject and how they keep it. I can find out the format. I can speak data-language and find out what I need to know to successfully request the data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: If this method does not work, my fallback is to ask first for their record layout and data dictionary in a request. Then, I actually request the data. I sometimes will also ask first how they keep the data, and in what system. That way, I can research the ways the data can be exported in advance of writing my request.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Plan ahead to save time: submit a formal access request whenever you set out to look for information. It's better not to wait until you have exhausted all other possibilities. You will save time by submitting a request at the beginning of your research and carrying out other investigations in parallel.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Think inside the filing cabinet: try to find out what data is collated. For example, if you get a blank copy of the form the police fill out after traffic accidents, you can then see what information they do or do not record about car crashes.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Be specific: before you submit your request, think: is it in any way ambiguous? If you ask for figures for 'the past three years', some authorities will send you information for the past three calendar years and others for the past three financial years, which you won't be able to directly compare.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Do a test run: if you are planning to send the same request to many public authorities, start by sending an initial draft of the request to a few authorities as a pilot exercise. This will show you whether you are using the right terminology to obtain the material you want and whether answering your questions is feasible.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: As with many projects that start with government data, the data needed a lot of cleaning. For instance, while there are only around 30 possible Advanced Placement courses, some schools reported having hundreds of them. This took lots of manual checking and phone calls to schools for confirmation and corrections.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Participants mostly used one dataset to discover news, instead of searching interconnections between different sources. The reason for this is that you need some statistical knowledge to verify news from data journalism.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: By systematically cross-referencing company names with spending datasets, it was possible to identify which companies had government contracts—which could then be followed up with FOI requests.
extracts.chapter: Covering the Public Purse with OpenSpending.org
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: News organizations are all about geography—and proximity to the news desk. If you're close, it's easy to suggest stories and become part of the process; conversely, out of sight is literally out of mind. Before WikiLeaks, we were placed on a different floor, with graphics. Since WikiLeaks, we have sat on the same floor, next to the newsdesk.
extracts.chapter: Behind the Scenes at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.notes: Simon Rogers on Guardian organizational shift
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Find government organizational charts and look for departments/units with a cross-cutting function (e.g., reporting, IT services), then explore their websites. A lot of data is kept in multiple departments and while for one, a particular database may be their crown jewels, another may give it to you freely.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Sometimes the data is already public and available, but no one has looked at it closely. In the case of the Associated Press's report on 4,500 pages of declassified documents, the material was obtained by an independent journalist over several years, using Freedom of Information requests. They scanned the paper results and uploaded them to DocumentCloud, which made it possible for us to do our comprehensive analysis.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: If you want to analyze, explore, or manipulate data using a computer, then you should explicitly ask for data in an electronic, machine-readable format. You may wish to clarify this by specifying, for example, that you require budgetary information in a format suitable for analysis with accounting software. You may also wish to explicitly ask for information in disaggregated or granular form.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: It is possible to find information about NGOs, private companies, religious organizations, and other organizations that are not required to release documents under FOI laws by asking public bodies which are covered by FOI laws. For example, you could ask a government department or ministry if they have funded or dealt with a specific private company or NGO and request supporting documents.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: I used FOI to ensure the data released under the UK transparency agenda is well-explained and contains what it should. FOI can be used not just to obtain data, but to request the schema of the database, the guidance workers receive when they work with the data, and the contract with the database provider.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Go wob-shopping. We have a fabulous diversity of freedom of information laws in Europe, and different countries have different political interests at different times. This can be used to your advantage. It greatly helped our case to point out to the Dutch authorities that the data was accessible in Denmark.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Don't take no for an answer when it's about transparency. Be persistent and follow a story over time. Things may well change and allow better reporting based upon better access at a later point.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Get out good raw material even if you find it in unexpected places and combine it with existing publicly accessible data. In this case the data had to be extracted from documents and sorted in databases. By augmenting the data with further data, analysis, and research, they were able to map numerous routes of the illegal weapon trade.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Comparing the records with comparable data from similar government entities can display deviations and shed light on probable corruption. Exaggerated and unusual expenses can be detected only by comparison. We put the data in spreadsheets, ran basic checks and comparisons among the municipalities and got maximum and minimum figures.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Machine-readable data is created for processing by a computer, instead of the presentation to a human user. The structure of such data relates to contained information, and not the way it is displayed eventually. Examples of easily machine-readable formats include CSV, XML, JSON, and Excel files, while formats like Word documents, HTML pages, and PDF files are more concerned with the visual layout of the information.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: The real complexity involved in scraping a website is in addressing the right pages and the right elements within these pages to extract the desired information. These tasks aren't about programming, but understanding the structure of the website and database.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Choosing the right programming language is largely a question of which community you have access to: if there is someone in your newsroom or city already working with one of these languages, then it makes sense to adopt the same language.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: The challenge with huge swathes of UK data isn't getting it released—it's getting it into a usable format. Using a service like ScraperWiki to ask coders to produce a scraper did around half of our job for us: we had all MPs' information in one sheet, ready for the lengthy task of analysing and cleaning. Services like this or tools such as Outwit Hub are a huge help to journalists trying to compile messy data who are unable to code themselves.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: If you go to whois.domaintools.com you can get the basic registration information for any website. You can also enter numerical IP addresses and get data on the organization or individual that owns that server. This is especially handy when you're trying to track down more information on an abusive or malicious user of a service.
extracts.chapter: The Web as a Data Source
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: It's common for scammers and spammers to astroturf their way towards legitimacy by building multiple sites that review and link to each other. They look like independent domains, and may even have different registration details, but they'll often actually live on the same server because that's a lot cheaper. Cohosted-with statistics give you an insight into the hidden business structure of the site you're researching.
extracts.chapter: The Web as a Data Source
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: TinEye offers a specialized reverse image search process, where you give it the image you have, and it finds other pictures on the web that look very similar. Because they use image recognition to do the matching, it even works when a copy has been cropped, distorted, or compressed. This can be extremely effective when you suspect that an image is being misrepresented, since it can lead back to the actual source.
extracts.chapter: The Web as a Data Source
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: When a page becomes controversial, the publishers may take it down or alter it without acknowledgment. The first place to turn is Google's cache of the page as it was when it did its last crawl. For longer time periods like months or years, the Internet Archive runs a service called The Wayback Machine that periodically takes snapshots of the most popular pages on the web.
extracts.chapter: The Web as a Data Source
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: Email headers work like postmarks, and can reveal a surprising amount about the sender. They often include the IP address of the machine that the email was sent from. You can then run whois on that IP number to find out which organization owns that machine. If it turns out to be a consumer ISP, you can visit MaxMind to get its approximate location.
extracts.chapter: The Web as a Data Source
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Sometimes you will get a ton of files, statistics, or reports which it is impossible for one person to go through. This is where crowdsourcing can help. A lot of the crowdsourcing we have done relies on help from obsessives. Making it more like a game can really help to engage people. Giving people specific tasks made a big difference because presenting people with a mountain of information to go through can make for hard and unrewarding work.
extracts.chapter: Crowdsourcing Data at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Regarding the reliability of crowdsourced data, the approach that Old Weather have got is really good. They get ten people to do each entry, which is a good way to ensure accuracy. The data we were getting out was not always reliable. Even though stories were great, it wasn't producing raw numbers that we could confidently use.
extracts.chapter: Crowdsourcing Data at the Guardian Datablog
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: You can't just think 'What do I want to know for my story?', you have to think 'What do people want to tell me right now?' It's only when you tap into what people want to talk about that crowdsourcing is going to be successful.
extracts.chapter: How the Datablog Used Crowdsourcing to Cover Olympic Ticketing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Have very specific things you want to know. Ask things that get multiple choice responses as much as possible. Try to get some basic demographics of who you are talking to so you can see if your sample might be biased. If you are asking for amounts, try in the guidance to specify that it's in digits, that they have to use a specific currency. And always add a comment box.
extracts.chapter: How the Datablog Used Crowdsourcing to Cover Olympic Ticketing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Rather than taking only valid responses, I tried to fix what I had. Some people had used foreign currencies, so I converted them to sterling. I knocked out the obviously silly entries and a few dozen who put in obviously fake high amounts to try to distort the results. That left me with a set that I could use with the normal data principles we use every day. I did a pivot table. I did some averaging.
extracts.chapter: How the Datablog Used Crowdsourcing to Cover Olympic Ticketing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: You have to start by thinking, 'What is the best tool for what I want to know?' Is it a comment thread? Or is it building an app? And if it is building an app, you have to think 'Is this worth the wait? And is it worth the resources that are required to do it?' Google Forms was chosen because if someone fills in the form, you can see the result as a row on a spreadsheet.
extracts.chapter: How the Datablog Used Crowdsourcing to Cover Olympic Ticketing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Data-literacy is the ability to consume for knowledge, produce coherently, and think critically about data. Data literacy includes statistical literacy, but also understanding how to work with large datasets, how they were produced, how to connect various datasets, and how to interpret them.
extracts.chapter: Become Data Literate in Three Simple Steps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: When in doubt about a number's credibility, always double-check, just as you'd have if it had been a quote from a politician. For police data, sociologists often carry out victimization studies, in which they ask people if they are subject to crime. These studies are much less volatile than police data. Other tests let you assess precisely the credibility of the data, such as Benford's law, but none will replace your own critical thinking.
extracts.chapter: Become Data Literate in Three Simple Steps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Always take the distribution and base rate into account. Checking for the mean and median, as well as mode helps you gain insights in the data. Knowing the order of magnitude makes contextualization easier. Reporting in natural frequencies (1 in 100) is way easier for readers to understand than using percentage (1%).
extracts.chapter: Become Data Literate in Three Simple Steps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: As a journalist, it makes little sense to challenge the numerical results of a study, such as the sample size, unless there are serious doubts about it. However, it is easy to see if researchers failed to take relevant pieces of information into account. If researchers don't control for lifestyle factors, they tell us nothing more than 'rich people are healthier.'
extracts.chapter: Become Data Literate in Three Simple Steps
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Think of data as something to play with and explore and it will often yield secrets and stories with surprising ease. Handle it simply as you'd handle other evidence, without fear or favor. Be creative by thinking of the alternative stories that might be consistent with the data and explain it better, then test them against more evidence. 'What other story could explain this?' is a handy prompt.
extracts.chapter: Tips for Working with Numbers in the News
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: If you believe in data, try to let it speak before you slap on your own mood, beliefs, or expectations. Data journalism adds little value if you are not open-minded. It is only as objective as you strive to make it, and not by virtue of being based on numbers.
extracts.chapter: Tips for Working with Numbers in the News
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Uncertainty is OK. We associate numbers with authority and certainty. Often the answer is that there is no answer, or the answer may be the best we have but still wouldn't hit a barn door for accuracy. There can often be more than one legitimate way of cutting the data. Numbers don't have to be either true or false.
extracts.chapter: Tips for Working with Numbers in the News
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: The investigation is a story. The story of how you tried to find out can make great journalism, as you go from one piece of evidence to another. Different sources provide new angles, new ideas, and richer understanding. I wonder if we're too hung up on wanting to be authoritative and tell people the answer—and so we miss a trick by not showing the sleuthing.
extracts.chapter: Tips for Working with Numbers in the News
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Data requests should begin with a list of questions you want to answer. Working with data is like interviewing a live source. You work backward. First, list the data-evidenced statements you want to make in your story. Then decide which variables and records you would have to acquire and analyze in order to make those statements.
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: It's often a good idea to request all the variables and records in the database, rather than the subset that could answer the questions for the immediate story. Getting all the data can be cheaper than getting a subset. You can always subset the data on your own, and having access to the full dataset will let you answer new questions that may come up.
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: One of the biggest problems in database work is that often you will be using data for analysis reasons that has been gathered for bureaucratic reasons. The standard of accuracy for those two is quite different. The first big piece of work when you acquire a new dataset is to examine how messy it is and then clean it up. A good quick way to look for messiness is to create frequency tables of the categorical variables.
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Data cleanup gets even more problematic when working with names. Are 'Joseph T. Smith', 'Joseph Smith', 'J.T. Smith', 'Jos. Smith', and 'Joe Smith' all the same person? It may take looking at other variables like address or date of birth, or even deeper research in other records, to decide. But tools like Google Refine can make the cleanup and standardization task faster and less tedious.
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: The Rosetta Stone of any database is the so-called data dictionary. It will tell you how the data file is formatted, the order of the variables, the names of each variable, and the datatype of each variable. The other key element is an explanation of any codes being used by particular variables. Without the data dictionary, these datasets could be difficult or even impossible to analyze properly.
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Always ask the agency giving you data if there are any undocumented elements in the data, whether it is newly created codes that haven't been included in the data dictionary, changes in the file layout, or anything else. Always examine the results of your analysis and ask 'Does this make sense?'
extracts.chapter: Basic Steps in Working with Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Do not assume that the background notes for government data will help explain what information is being presented or that the people responsible for the data will realize the data is not clear even when you tell them your mistaken assumption. Journalists have a responsibility to check ridiculous claims, even if it means that this drops the story down the news list.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: To draw your readers in, you have to be able to hit them with a headline figure that makes them sit up and take notice. You should almost be able to read the story without having to know that it comes from a dataset. We approached the dataset with specific queries in mind, looking for key terms like 'cocktail', 'golf', and 'away days'.
extracts.chapter: Start With the Data, Finish With a Story
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Another approach is to start with a blacklist and look for exclusions. An easy way to pull storylines from data is to know what you shouldn't find in there. We queried the data based on the Commission's own rules about what kinds of companies should be prohibited from receiving structural funds.
extracts.chapter: Start With the Data, Finish With a Story
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: A typology of different kinds of data stories includes: Measurement (counting or totaling), Proportion, Internal comparison, External comparison, Change over time, League tables, Analysis by categories, and Association. Always remember that correlation and causation are not the same thing.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: I don't think that all reporters need to know how to code. But I do think it is very valuable for them to have a more general awareness of what is possible and know how to talk to coders. My advice is to learn Excel and do some simple stories first. Start out small and work your way up to database analysis and mapping.
extracts.chapter: Data Journalists Discuss Their Tools of Choice
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: With respect to interpreting data: don't take this lightly. You have to be conscientious. Pay attention to detail and question your results. Keep notes on how you're processing the data and keep a copy of the original data. It is easy to make a mistake. I always do my analysis two or three times practically from scratch. Even better would be to get your editor or someone else to analyze the data separately and compare the results.
extracts.chapter: Data Journalists Discuss Their Tools of Choice
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: What's really nice about R is that you're able to keep an exact protocol of what you're doing with the data throughout the entire process—from reading a CSV file to generating charts. If the data changes, you can regenerate the chart using one click. If someone is curious about the integrity of your chart, you can show the exact source, which allows everyone to recreate the exact chart on their own.
extracts.chapter: Data Journalists Discuss Their Tools of Choice
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: It is unrealistic to expect that data visualization tools and techniques will unleash a barrage of ready-made stories from datasets. There are no rules, no protocol that will guarantee us a story. Instead, it makes more sense to look for insights, which can be artfully woven into stories in the hands of a good journalist.
extracts.chapter: Using Data Visualization to Find Insights in Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Start the documentation by writing down initial thoughts before taking your first look at the data. This helps us to identify our bias and reduces the risk of misinterpretation of the data by just finding what we originally wanted to find. The documentation is the most important step of the process. Charts are only valuable if presented in the context in which they have been created.
extracts.chapter: Using Data Visualization to Find Insights in Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Possible data transformations include: Zooming to look at a certain detail; Aggregation to combine many data points into a single group; Filtering to temporarily remove data points not in major focus; and Outlier removal to get rid of single points not representative for 99% of the dataset.
extracts.chapter: Using Data Visualization to Find Insights in Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Visualization and data wrangling should be easy and cheap. If changing parameters of the visualizations takes you hours, you won't experiment that much. It often makes a lot of sense to choose a tool that covers both the data wrangling and the data visualization issues. Separating the tasks in different tools means that you have to import and export your data very often.
extracts.chapter: Using Data Visualization to Find Insights in Data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: We try to pick tools that anyone could get the hang of without learning a programming language or having special training and without a hefty fee attached. All the datasets we tidy and release are available as a Google Spreadsheet, which means people with a Google account can download the data, import it into their own account and make their own charts.
extracts.chapter: Data Journalists Discuss Their Tools of Choice
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Bad data visualization is worse in many respects than none at all. With the barrier to entry now barely a speed bump, the question facing journalists is now less about whether you can turn your dataset into a visualization, but whether you should.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Our workflow usually starts in Excel. It is such an easy way to quickly work out if there's something interesting in the data. If we have a sense that there is something in it, then we go to the news desk. Then we look at how we should visualize it or show it on the page. Then we write the post that goes with it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: We often will embed our data onto our site in a visualization and in a form that allows for easy download of the dataset. It increases the transparency of The Seattle Times. We are showing the readers the same data that we used to draw powerful conclusions. By making the data available we also can enlist tips from these same critics and general readers on what we may have missed.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Giving news consumers easy access to the data we use for our work is the right thing to do. Readers can assure themselves that we aren't torturing the data to reach unfair conclusions. Opening up our data is in the social science tradition of allowing researchers to replicate our work. Encouraging readers to study the data can generate tips that may lead to follow-up stories.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Foundational epistemological principle remains essential in any technological era

extracts.extract: Know your scope. There's a big difference between hacking for fun and engineering for scale and performance. Make sure you've partnered with people who have the appropriate skill set for your project. Don't forget design. Usability, user experience, and presentation design can greatly affect the success of your project.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Any discussion about how to build a great news app should start with a product development mentality: keep a laser focus on the user. News apps don't serve the story for its own sake—they serve the user. Always be aware of the people who will be using it. Concentrate on designing and building features based on their needs.
extracts.chapter: How to Build a News App
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Developers in the newsroom are like water in the desert: highly sought-after and in short supply. Just because a story lends itself to a complex, beautiful news app doesn't mean you need to build one. Balancing priorities is critical. Every app you build comes at a cost: namely, another potentially more impactful app you could have been working on instead.
extracts.chapter: How to Build a News App
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: A news app should tell a story, and just like any good news story, it needs a headline, a byline, a lead, and a nut graph. A news app should be generative, meaning it should generate more stories and more reporting. We tend to build news apps when we have a dataset that is national in scope yet granular enough to expose meaningful details.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: We follow a modified Agile methodology to build our apps. We iterate quickly and show drafts to the other folks in the newsroom. Most importantly, we work really closely with reporters and read their drafts. We work much more like reporters than like traditional programmers. In addition to writing code, we call sources, gather information, and build expertise.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: In the reporting phase, visualizations can: help you identify themes and questions for the rest of your reporting; identify outliers—good stories, or perhaps errors, in your data; help you find typical examples; and show you holes in your reporting. Start early and often with visualizations in your reporting. Don't consider it a separate step, something to be considered after the story is largely written.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Use small multiples to quickly orient yourself in a large dataset. This was really easy to see in a series of graphs rather than a table of numbers, and an interactive form let us check various types of grants, regions and agencies. Maps in small multiples can be a way to show time and place on a static image that's easy to compare.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: When you're trying to understand a story or a dataset, there's no wrong way to look at it; try it every way you can think of. Use raw numbers, percentages, and indexes. Look at them on different scales. Try following the rule that the x-axis must be zero. Then break that rule and see if you learn more. Try out logarithms and square roots for data with odd distributions.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: If you want to publish anything based on your early exploration, you have to resolve questions and you can't make assumptions. They're either interesting stories or mistakes. First, walk back your own work. Have you read the documentation, its caveats and does the problem exist in the original version of the data? If everything on your end seems right, then it's time to pick up the phone.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Your exploratory graphics should be generally correct, but don't worry if you have various levels of rounding, if they don't add up to exactly 100 percent or if you are missing one or two years' data out of 20. This is part of the exploration process. You'll still see the big trends and know what you have to collect before it's time for publication.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: At the start of any complex story, begin building chronologies of key events and cases. You can use Excel, a Word document, or a special tool like TimeFlow for the task, but at some point you will find a dataset you can layer behind. Reading through it periodically will show you what holes are in your reporting that have to be filled out.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Brainstorm about possible graphics with the artists and designers in your newsroom. They will have good ways to look at your data, suggestions of how it might work interactively, and know how to connect data and stories. It will make your reporting much easier if you know what you have to collect early on.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: There's no hiding in an interactive graphic. If you are really going to have your readers explore the data any way they want, then every data element has to be what it claims to be. If you're building your own database, you should expect to proofread, fact check, and copyedit the entire database.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: The graphic should satisfy two different kinds of readers. It should be easy to understand at a glance, but complex enough to offer something interesting to people who want to go further. Decide on the overwhelming impression you want a reader to get, and make everything else disappear.
extracts.chapter: Visualization as the Workhorse of Data Journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Data visualization leverages a powerful cognitive advantage: fully half of the human brain is devoted to processing visual information. A well-designed data visualization can give viewers an immediate and profound impression, and cut through the clutter of a complex story to get right to the point. Data visualization is deeply rooted in measurable facts—less emotionally charged, more concerned with shedding light than heat.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: When not to use data visualization: when your story can be better told through text or multimedia; when you have very few data points; when you have very little variability in your data, no clear trend, or conclusion; when the spatial element is not meaningful; when a table would do.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: As a journalist, the most important aspect of working with the data is that you first choose the story you are interested in telling. Both grouped and stacked bar charts are valid displays of the same information, but they are two different stories using the same starting data. The story will guide the choice of visualization.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Bars are used for discrete units of measurement, whereas lines are used when it is a continuous value. It is OK to not use every piece of data in your story. You shouldn't have to ask permission to be concise, it should be the rule. Starting small is the most effective way to tell the story, slowly building only when needed.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: A good visualization is like a good picture. You understand what it is about just by looking at it for a moment or two. The more you look at the visual, the more you see. The visualization is bad when the reader does not know where to start or where to stop, and when the visualization is overloaded by details.
extracts.chapter: How We Serve Data at Verdens Gang
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: When visualizing data, it is important to understand the level of data literacy of our users. Complex diagrams and interactive applications might not meaningfully communicate to our users. A good visualization will speak to the user in a language they can understand, and bring forth a story that they can easily connect with.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Publish the raw data alongside your articles. Either host the data in comma-separated plain text, or host it in a third-party service like Google Docs. That way, there's only one version of the data around, and you can update it as necessary if you find errors. The most important thing you can do with your data is share it as widely and openly as possible.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: Keep an eye on metrics on which parts of your datasets are getting attention—it's likely that the most trafficked areas could have something to say that you might have missed. Think beyond the comment box. Can you attach comments to particular cells in a spreadsheet? Or a particular region of an infographic?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: As a rule of thumb: if data is published by government, you should neither ask forgiveness nor permission; if it is published by an organization that doesn't make money selling data, you shouldn't have to worry too much; if it is published by an organization that does make money from selling data, then you should definitely ask permission.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates
